steps.title: Identify what the sentence needs
steps.content:
The passage sets up a conditional: if clustered camera trapping and GPS collar tracking underestimates cougar density, that would explain why a specific study’s result is low. The blank must name a study that (1) used those methods and (2) reported a notably low density.
  • Choice A — P. Ian Ross and Martin G. Jalkotzy reported a maximum density of 4.70 individuals per 100 square kilometers, while the study by Richard Beausoleil et al., which used biopsy darting, reported a lower maximum density.: This compares two studies and mentions biopsy darting — a different method entirely. It does not name a study that used the methods in question and produced a low result. ✗
  • Choice B — Sean M. Murphy et al. reported a maximum density of only 1.65 individuals per 100 square kilometers, lower than that reported by several other studies.: Murphy et al. used clustered camera trapping and GPS collar tracking and reported 1.65 — the lowest density in the table. This directly supports the underestimate claim. ✓
  • Choice C — Richard A. Beausoleil et al. required a study area of 7,939 square kilometers, while the study by Sean M. Murphy et al. required a study area of 4,329 square kilometers.: Study area size is irrelevant to the density-underestimate argument; this choice addresses geography, not methodology or results. ✗
  • Choice D — Lindsey N. Rich et al. reported a maximum density of 6.50 individuals per 100 square kilometers, higher than that reported by several other studies.: A high density is the opposite of what an underestimate would produce. ✗

steps.title: Lock in the answer
steps.content:
Only Murphy et al. used the exact methods named in the passage and recorded the lowest density figure, which is what an underestimate would look like.

That matches Choice B — Sean M. Murphy et al. reported a maximum density of only 1.65 individuals per 100 square kilometers, lower than that reported by several other studies..
